sentences.text: Yes, yes.
My father was a-- there wasn't an official judge.
If there was some trouble between people, he was always called in to-- and I don't know if you know what the word 'guber' means.
They're a so-called 'guber,' you know, he was kind of one of the people who got called in to do, to make peace or to judge or to--
Yes.
Yeah, no, they did call them for non-Jewish too.
Yes.
Yeah, there wasn't-- there wasn't such a-- there weren't enough people for two sections.
So you know, you got called in to judge between two people fighting over two inches of land or the cow wandered off to somebody else's field or, he used to go to help as a judgment in the peace.
We dealt in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to explain.
If I say hotel, it sounds very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had rooms where people used to come and stay.
It was, as I said before, it was a holiday place.
It was like a hotel today but in a much smaller and much-- and he also dealt with vine and drinks, imported, also a very fancy word.
He used to buy up-- he used to go to Hungary and buy up so many acres of grapes and had them made into wine.
And we supplied the vine for the town, kosher wine.
Everybody came to us to buy.
We had cellars with barrels and that kind of thing.
That was his main occupation.
But I think he was also a land owner.
Quite a lot of the houses belonged to the family, you know, all the Birenbaums and so--
It was like a biggish bungalow.
And we had a very big garden.
And at the end of the garden, there was a land which was always rented out to a non-Jewish family.
And they lived on that land, and they worked the land.
And half the products of the land went to this family and half to us.
That was the payment.
You didn't pay for mining land.
You shared the profits of the-- but that was only for-- for family use.
That was enough.
There was cows and goats and chickens and whatever.
And that was enough to provide us for the food continuously.
And then they were harvesting and putting away for the winter because the winters used to be tremendously bad, very, very cold.
So if you didn't put away food in the summer in the-- for the winter, you starved.
In cellars.
And we also had another business, which was preserving ice.
So all the-- all the winter while the river was frozen, we employed everybody who had a horse and cart.
And they went to the river and cut the ice, brought it into our specially-built cellar, which was full with hay.
So we put ice and hay, ice and hay on top of the other.
And then we used to sell it in the summer.
People used to come with a bucket to buy a bucket of ice.
And then we had other cellars which kept the potato, onion, cabbage, all those things.
And then, at the end of summer, we used to have a summer house.
And women used to come to preserve, you know, make the-- all the jam for the winter and all the tomatoes and-- everything was homemade.
You couldn't buy a jar of jam.
There was no such a thing.
There wasn't even a shop to buy it from.
There were shops, but everything was homemade and preserved for the winter.
And that's how-- that was always very exciting that the women-- everything was exciting because as a child, everything gets exciting.
And also nothing was happening.
There was-- I remember the first picture house came to town because we weren't allowed to go because [INAUDIBLE] people just don't go to pictures.
So we used to look through the keyhole and do all sorts of things just to get a glimpse of this black and white silent films.
[LAUGHS] I always think-- I always remember the Shirley Temple.
[LAUGHS] And so anything like that, like the corn harvest-- we used to have people specially employed for the corn harvest.
And then there was a big urn in the garden, and all the corn was put in there with the fresh ones.
And you eat at corn all day long, you know.
And the same with other things.
These women came for-- five women-- and they boiled the tomatoes in huge, big urns and then sift them into bottles and preserve them for over the winter.
And then the strawberries were ready.
They were picked and so that was always for us children.
You know, it was always the event.
And this event happened all summer-- spring and summer, winter.
No, we just enjoyed all the--
--all the things.
We did-- we did have our-- everybody had their own little garden near the house.
We were very encouraged to grow things.
Like, I had a little vegetable patch and a little-- the fruit trees.
I used to sit-- climb on the fruit trees.
You know, it-- it was a real country life, which is such a shame.
It's missing these days.
It was really, very, very nice.
And we learned a lot.
You know, I don't think my children would know how-- how the corn grows or the lettuce grows.
And it was-- I remember sitting in the middle of the carrot patch, doing homework, and pulling it out, wiping the carrot and eating it.
I don't think you could do that today.
[LAUGHS]
And these-- these things which kept children happy, and that was the exciting part.
Oh, yes.
Yes, probably more than a bigger town because you are kind of on top of each other and-- and family is involved and land involved and business involved, and I think there was a lot of controversy.
No, no, no, no, no.
You see, children those days weren't allowed to kind of listening to parents.
If parents talked, if adults talked, the children have to leave the room, which now I am very sorry because there are so many things missing I don't know.
I only know that must have been something on because they closed the door.
But what was going on I don't know.
Maybe, if I would have been-- I think my age was just an awkward age.
I wasn't young enough and I wasn't old enough.
It's just when you observe things, but you really don't know.
Because I know from my cousins who I say are five, six, seven years older than me, they know what was happening.
You know, they know more because of older.
I was just at that age when I wasn't really interested either.
It's just that there was an argument.
It was very interesting there was an argument.
I wasn't really concerned what the argument was about.
So it's a shame that my age was-- I wasn't-- I wasn't old enough to realize--
That's right.
That's right.
I don't know that it would have been beneficial to me, but it would have been interesting.
Always.
I mean, this is a bit far-fetched what I'm going to say.
If you coughed, you didn't call the doctor.
You entered the lab to the rebbe.
But, you know-- an inverted comma-- yes, yes, it was-- they used to go off.
But life around the rebbe, I mean, even the local rebbe-- Friday night when we finished eating, the boys-- my brothers and my father-- used to go there for benching and used to go there for kiddish.
Everything centered around the rebbe's house, which was next door to our house.
So the Belzer rebbe was also if it-- that was-- had to be a bit more serious if you went to there because it-- I don't know how far it was, but those days, everything was far.
Very little.
I think for my bat mitzvah present, I was 12 and my father took me up to Budapest, which was a terrific treat.
I was 12, and I had to travel through the night by train.
At night, by train.
I mean, it was really exciting.
I was so ill on that train.
I never traveled, and I was-- I was really ill.
And you know, you cannot use the traveling.
And it was very exciting.
And it was fantastic, especially because I made everybody else in town jealous, and that was the fun.
[LAUGHS]
I just remember big, big, big.
You see, we had one building which had more than just flat.
The doctor's building had an upper floor, which I've never-- that was the only high building I've ever seen.
Everybody lived in level, one-level, like bungalow.
And here, there was five stories, six stories.
It was-- it was tremendous.
And the tram and the bus-- it-- it-- it was very impressive, very, very, impressive.
Very exciting and eating in a restaurant, you know.
It was really very, very exciting.
And funny enough, I didn't crave for it.
I didn't say, oh, I wish I would go and live there because it never came into question.
But I did enjoy it.
And the next day at school, everybody was just, you know, what was it and how long-- what did you-- is it true?
Is it true?
Is it true?
You know, like, all sorts of questions.
And I remember bringing home books.
I remember the Dickens books, and it was like we sat and read and-- and, you know, there was no library.
And my father said, you can buy books.
And my aunts gave me that.
And we came home, and that was the big excitement, you know.
We could read books.
I think it was Dickens, the first really permissible book which were allowed to read apart from the Jewish books and school books.
And-- and I was-- I was the big hero because the book belonged to me.
And I could lend it to this and that and the other.
And that has permission to lend it to the next one.
You know, it was-- it was-- I was a big man.
[LAUGHS]
My parents.
It was my parents, yes.
Bat mitzvah.
I was 12, yes.
And I got to watch them, and these were big events in ones life.
Nothing.
The bar mitzvah?
No, no.
It was just happened to-- happened to be at that time of the year when it was convenient to go to Budapest.
So they just said, you know, this is it.
You're 12 now.
We will take you.
When I was there, when I lived with them, he was big and strong and very-- I didn't ever imagine not to have a protection like him.
He was very tall and quite a big built man and very authoritative, very, you know, old father' way.
You know, it was this.
And I loved him and dreaded him at the same time because if he came into the room and if you were guilty of anything, we really shook because it was-- and-- and he was very respected in town too.
So it was confirmed that I was right to be afraid of him or whatever and to think that he is really somebody because all that advice they needed in town, they used to come to us.
Not all, but to me it was all.

Probably there were other people who gave advise, but I remember people coming and closing the door because they-- they needed money or they needed a doctor or they needed whatever or they needed to make peace between two people.
And I watched that, I mean.
It was only my father could do that, I thought.
Probably some other people could do it.
But on the other hand, my mother was very quiet and very fine and small and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the love between was-- between them was very, very great and a respect for each other.
And I think that gives you a lot of security.
She was very gentle, and you know, my father was annoyed.
He was-- she was always there to protect us.
And I remember we would love to sleep in her bed.
And she was very gentle and the completely opposite of my father.
She came from Yugoslavia.
She was born in a place called Zenta.
But when she was about two, her father died.
So my grandmother went back to Hungary to her original birth place.
And she-- she had a brother living there, my grandmother.
And they had three children.
So my grandmother moved-- moved in with this family, this is-- her brother.
And my mother was brought up with these three other children.
And that's where she got married from.
She always-- my grandmother never remarried.
That was the place where I used to go every summer for four or five weeks.
She was a well-to-do lady.
She had a lot of property and land and spoiled me rotten.
Used to have a private tutor for the summer, and all my clothes were made there.
My mother was the only daughter, and I was the only granddaughter obviously because-- and she was a very, very, very loving grandmother.
Her name was Pearl Pollack, but her maiden name was Klein.
And she used to come to us, I think, three or four times a year.
And every week, I used to get the long letters.
Every Thursday there was a parcel from her because she lived in Hungary where food was in abundance.
And in our place in the winter, food wasn't-- it was shortage of-- because of the cold, the climate.
Every Thursday, we used to go to the station to get these big crate of food she used to send us.
She used to write a letter for every Shabbos.
And I never remember a letter ever-- there was a ribbon in it or a little handkerchief in it or, you know, a very, very sweet old-- old lady.
I mean, she was younger than me.
To me then, she was a very sweet old lady.
And I used to stay there for four or five weeks being spoiled, very bored.
And then, for four weeks-- usually we had two months holidays in the summer.
So for the last following four weeks, the second four, we used to go into the mountains and see these cousins who lived in the same town.
They had a mountain house.
And we used to go there because as hot or cold the winter was, the summer was just as hot because it was a meadow that was surrounded with mountains, and the summer was very, very hot.
It was like living in a-- in a, you know, the snow wind because it was surrounded with all these mountains so it was very, very-- spend four weeks in the mountains, yes.
They were mischievous.
They were naughty, as naughty go then.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had it tough because my father was very, very, very, very strict with them.
Very strict.
And, you know, when some children were playing, they always had to sit and learn.
And they were young.
It's not like in the older when you have the choice or you enjoy it.
I don't know they enjoyed it.
And one was seems was a mathematical genius.
I mean, I don't know how they found out then what mathematical genius.
And the other one was just a good sport and sweet.
They were young.
One was bar mitzvahed just before we left.
The older one.
Szmul.
And he was-- he was bar mitzvahed which was a saddest occasion because it was already very bad.
And anything celebrated, it was very sad.
And the second one wasn't even-- he left before he was bar mitzvahed.
It wasn't like it is today.
I mean, to us it was a big deal.
The family came.
Some family came from out of town, out of town, probably the next village.
And they baked a lot.
And after shul everybody came to our house and had kiddish, and that was it.
Yes.
That was it.
That was-- but it was very-- I remember that time I was allowed to go to shul.
And it was very, very sad.
I just remember everybody is crying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because?
I don't even think they knew the reason, but they knew that there was probably the last bar mitzvah.
And it was very, very, very hard.
And, you know, they tried to make it as jolly as possible for his sake.
But it was-- it was-- I can't even explain it.
It wasn't tragic.
It was just sad.
I don't even know if my grandmother was there.
I think she was there because traveling was already for Jews wasn't allowed and things were bad.
So that was-- that was the bar mitzvah.
In our family, it was quite a big thing because the direct family had no other sons.
So on my-- you know, my near aunt-- we had a lot of aunts and uncles, dozens and dozens.
But we were half brothers and half sisters.
And the real connection was with this one aunt who lived in town.
They had no sons so this was the big event-- the first bar mitzvah in the family.
Mammerstein.
Rose Mammerstein.
They owned mineral waters.
They had mineral-- and they bottled them and exported them all over Europe.
It was supposed to have been good for the stomach.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's where I used to play and go.
But we had lots and lots and lots of aunts and uncles and cousins, cousins who were older than my father.
Yeah.
So there was a lot of generation.
My generation was the third generation I was friendly with.
They were my age because there was such a big age gap between the brothers and the sisters.
But these were living near us, and my father had another sister who was living in Hungary.
And she used to come every summer.
And those-- there were four, two brothers and two sisters-- and they were the-- we were the closest together, even after the war there.

That's where I ended up with one of-- his two of the sisters together and-- and that's-- they remain to me the only relations-- one from-- the two sisters' children, they kind of looked after me when I came back from the camp.
1939 the Hungarians took over, took away the businesses, took away the property, and things got very bad.
We were a little bit longer better off because, we thought, because my father fought in the first world war.
So he had a lot of metals.
And so we thought that that-- we did benefit a little bit.
Not benefit, but we could keep the business a bit longer than the other Jews who-- and then they started-- My father went up to Budapest to try to get papers.
And, you know, try to do something to make it illegal for us to become part of this setup.
But it didn't succeed.
It's-- after three months later they took our land away and then the business away.
And that was that.
That was the beginning of the end-- the four years of hard struggle.
Not really, not as a child.
There was.
There was, but it was children.
You know, even if the child shouted at you 'Dirty Jew,' and 'Smelly Jew' or whatever, you know, it was a part of life.
It-- it wasn't-- if you are a child, you don't take it seriously.
Only when it started to be abusing-- physical and-- and started throwing stones at you and-- and, you know, in winter the snow used to be very, very deep in banks.
So you would have to clear it.
Sometimes we used to come out of the house, and the neighbor's snow was piled up on front of our house.
You know, things like that, and you couldn't get out.
I mean, these were little children things, but they were physically hardships.
It was very, very hard to live with that.
But then, it started to be physical hardship.
There wasn't enough food.
We were thrown out of the school.
If a child bust you up on the street, you didn't matter.
Nobody came to your rescue except your father or brother or whatever.
The boys never went to shul on their own any more, always accompanied by my father.
And sometimes, they cut the electricity and, you know, little things more and more and more and more.
And then in 1942, we started getting the people who were coming over the border and telling us these horror stories about Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember two girls walked in the middle of the night.
And they started telling us stories of what's happening in Poland.
And these are young girls, and they sat on our bed and telling us these stories.
They dig trenches, and they shoot you in.
And they come and take people away.
And they kill them.
And-- horror stories.
And my father overheard it, and he came into the room.
And I only just realized the significance of it-- how wrong-- how bad it was that we never believed it.
My father said, I don't want you to tell these stories for my children.
And then when the girls left the room, he said, don't listen because they are completely mad.
These things can't happen.
It's an imagination.
The next day, we looked at this girls.
How dare they tell us these stories.
So just to show how things were happening literally a few miles away, and we didn't know, and we didn't believe it.
He didn't believe it.
He did not believe it.
Nobody believed it.
Nobody believed it there.
Nobody moved.
There wasn't a person who left our town from being frightened.
There was this attitude it can't happen to me.
And it's only over the borders.
It can never come over the border.
But I think that is a human-- you don't want to believe it because-- and if you believe it, what will you do?
It wasn't like in Germany.
There was a transport.
You could go.
Well, we couldn't-- there was nowhere to go.
But maybe if you would have believed it, we would have made a bigger effort.
But we didn't.
We just did not believe it.
Yes, they heard about the Nazi party, but they didn't know it was that bad.
Once they came in, once they invaded our town, we knew it was bad.
But not that bad.
I mean, going to Auschwitz wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even on the way to Auschwitz, we wouldn't believe, you know, what was happening.
But I don't know if it's human nature which makes you not believe or-- or really we didn't believe.
Or we didn't want to believe it.
But, 1942, I remember those girls coming over.
Lots of people came over.
And they all-- lots of them came to our house and telling us stories.
And just would not believe it.
Well, maybe we believed it, but it was happening to them there, not to us here because we were over the border.
That happened on the other side of the border.
So, you know, it's-- it-- it was-- people say, why didn't you run?
Why didn't you know?
Why didn't you do something?
We didn't believe it, so we didn't do it.
And in any case, I don't think there was anywhere to run.
We were too far near the border.
There was nowhere to run.
Which way would we have run?
There was no-- I don't see there was anywhere we could have run.
Even if we would have hidden-- looking back on it, so we could have gone into the mountains and hide.
We couldn't have survived.
Nobody hid there and survived.
So who knows.
There was a lot of physical hardship because parents-- children couldn't go to school, children didn't have no shoes, the-- the firewood was little, the food was little, and the army was there.
We had a very, very, very big army there and also a Jewish army.
Yeah.
A Jewish army who was called a working army, but they were Jewish men.
And there was a lot of them in our town.
And we were allowed to have them, like, in for Pesach for the Seder.
And I had these cousins who-- where my mother lived-- those two were in this army.
And I used to take them food.
And it cheered-- it cheered up the town.
It was a little bit of a-- it was alive.
And a lot of the men from Siberia, the Jewish army, came-- had to come through our town.
And we went to the station.
We met them there.
They had to get off.
They couldn't go further.
I met quite a few people who used to-- who still remember that they came to us after the war.
OK.
